And so, you know, as a reminder, that's like the primary purpose of a registered agent is if you get sued or state notices, it needs to have a physical address with a person or a company that receives it on your behalf.
And you have to have a registered agent in every state where you do business.
And the basic reason, the fundamental reason for that is because states want to, they have several things they want to do.
They want to be.
They're able to know where they can sue you.
Like if somebody has a complaint and like needs to take legal action, they want to have a guaranteed way to do that.
And then same thing with state notices, but also they want to make sure that they have up-to-date information on your business for people in the state.
That brings us into the second part of our business.
So like when you form a company in Delaware, you have a registered agent, then you have any report filing requirements there.
And every state that you do business in in the U.S., you need to register in that state called foreign registration.
Foreign qualification, there's tons of different names for it, authority to transact business, but generally known as foreign registrations or registering to your business in the state.
And what the whole point of that is the same thing.
Tell the state a way to find you and then keep your information up to date.
So the state will periodically require you to file things and your reports, we call them internally periodic reports, but that's like, it's definitely internal nomenclature.
And the reason why we do that is because some of them are not annual, as you know, like New York's every...
